instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# New Developer Onboarding: Backend Services

Welcome to the backend team. This document covers everything you need to set up your development environment and make your first contribution.

Prerequisites: Go 1.25+ installed (we use the latest stable), Docker Desktop for running dependencies, and Git with SSH key configured for GitHub access. We use VS Code with the Go extension (gopls) as the recommended editor, though some team members use Neovim with nvim-lspconfig.

Environment setup:
1. Clone the repo: git clone git@github.com:example/api-service.git
2. Copy .env.example to .env and fill in local values
3. Start dependencies: docker compose up -d (starts PostgreSQL 16, Redis 7, and Ollama)
4. Run migrations: go run cmd/migrate/main.go up
5. Seed test data: go run cmd/seed/main.go
6. Run the server: go run cmd/api/main.go
7. Verify: curl http://localhost:8080/v2/health should return {"status":"ok"}

Project structure: cmd/ for entrypoints, internal/ for business logic (not importable externally), pkg/ for shared utilities, migrations/ for database migrations, deploy/ for Kubernetes manifests and Helm charts, api/proto/ for protobuf definitions.

First task recommendation: pick a "good first issue" from the GitHub issue tracker. These are typically small bug fixes or test additions that help you learn the codebase without risk.

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Pull Request Process

All code changes go through pull requests on GitHub. No direct pushes to main.

Before opening a PR:
1. Ensure your branch is rebased on the latest main
2. Run go test ./... locally — all tests must pass
3. Run golangci-lint run — no new lint warnings
4. If you changed the API, update the OpenAPI spec in api/openapi.yaml
5. If you added a new package, add it to the dependency graph in docs/architecture.md

PR requirements:
- Title follows conventional commits (feat:, fix:, docs:, refactor:, test:, chore:)
- Description explains WHAT changed and WHY, not HOW (the code shows how)
- Linked to a GitHub issue if applicable
- At least one approval from a team member
- All CI checks pass (lint, unit tests, integration tests, build)

CI pipeline runs on GitHub Actions. Typical run time: 4-6 minutes. If CI fails, check the failed step's logs. Common failures: lint errors (run golangci-lint locally), test failures (run the specific test with -v flag), and Docker build failures (usually a missing dependency in go.mod).

Merge strategy: we use squash merge to keep the main branch history clean. The PR title becomes the commit message on main. After merge, the feature branch is automatically deleted.

Code review expectations: reviewers should respond within 1 business day. If a review is blocking, ping in the #dev Slack channel.

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Go Coding Standards

These standards apply to all Go code in the project. New code must comply. Existing code should be updated when touched.

Formatting: use gofmt (enforced by CI). Line length is not strictly enforced but keep it reasonable. Use goimports for import sorting (stdlib, blank line, external, blank line, internal).

Naming:
- Packages: short, lowercase, no underscores. Avoid generic names (util, common, helpers)
- Exported types: PascalCase with descriptive names (UserRepository, not UserRepo)
- Interfaces: single-method interfaces use the -er suffix (Reader, Writer, Closer). Multi-method interfaces describe the capability (UserStore, not UserStorer)
- Errors: var ErrNotFound = errors.New("not found"). Prefix with Err. Wrap with fmt.Errorf("context: %w", err)

Structure:
- Keep functions under 50 lines. If longer, extract helper functions
- Prefer returning early (guard clauses) over deep nesting
- Use table-driven tests for any test with 3+ cases
- Every exported function needs a doc comment
- Avoid init() functions — they make testing and dependency injection harder

Concurrency:
- Always pass context.Context as the first parameter
- Use errgroup for parallel work, not raw goroutines
- Never use sync.Mutex when a channel would be clearer
- Protect shared state with sync.RWMutex when reads greatly outnumber writes

Dependencies: prefer stdlib over external packages. Before adding a dependency, check if stdlib has an equivalent. New dependencies require team review.

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Design System Documentation: Component Library

Our design system is built on Radix UI primitives with Tailwind CSS for styling. Components are documented and tested in Storybook. The design tokens (colors, spacing, typography) are defined in tailwind.config.ts and shared between web and mobile.

Color system: we use a semantic color palette with background, foreground, primary, secondary, muted, accent, destructive, and border tokens. Each token has light and dark mode variants defined as CSS custom properties. The dark mode is toggled by a .dark class on the html element.

Typography scale: 6 levels from xs (12px) to 3xl (30px). Font family: Inter for body text, JetBrains Mono for code. Line heights are set per size for optimal readability.

Component library structure:
- Primitives: Button, Input, Select, Checkbox, Radio, Switch (from Radix UI)
- Layout: Card, Sheet, Dialog, Popover, Tooltip (from Radix UI)
- Composite: DataTable (TanStack Table + our styling), CommandPalette (cmdk), Toast (Sonner)
- Navigation: Sidebar, Breadcrumb, Tabs, NavigationMenu

Accessibility: all components meet WCAG 2.1 AA standards. Radix UI provides keyboard navigation, focus management, and screen reader support out of the box. We test with axe-core in our Storybook integration.

New components: propose in the #design Slack channel. Requires design review before implementation. Must include Storybook story, TypeScript props interface, and Playwright visual regression test.

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Incident Response Process

When something breaks in production, follow this process. Speed matters but so does communication.

Severity levels:
- P1: Service is down or data loss is occurring. Wake the on-call engineer. Target response: 5 minutes.
- P2: Feature degradation affecting many users. Notify on-call via PagerDuty. Target response: 30 minutes.
- P3: Minor issue affecting few users. Create a ticket, address next business day.
- P4: Cosmetic or low-impact issue. Add to the backlog.

Incident commander role: the first responder becomes the incident commander until explicitly handed off. The IC coordinates response, communicates updates, and ensures nothing is forgotten.

Communication:
- Status updates every 15 minutes in the #incidents Slack channel for P1/P2
- External status page (hosted on Statuspage.io) updated within 10 minutes of a confirmed P1
- Post-incident email to affected customers within 24 hours for P1 incidents

Post-incident:
- Blameless post-mortem within 48 hours for all P1 and P2 incidents
- Document in the incidents folder: timeline, root cause, what went well, what to improve, action items
- Each action item gets a GitHub issue with the "incident-followup" label
- Track action item completion in the weekly team sync

Tools: PagerDuty for alerting, Slack for coordination, Grafana for dashboards, Statuspage.io for external communication.

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Git Branching Strategy

We use trunk-based development with short-lived feature branches. The main branch is always deployable.

Branching rules:
- Feature branches: feature/<ticket-id>-<short-description> (e.g., feature/PROJ-123-add-search-api)
- Bugfix branches: fix/<ticket-id>-<short-description>
- Branches must be created from the latest main
- Branches should live for no more than 3 days (smaller, more frequent PRs)
- Rebase onto main before opening a PR (no merge commits in PRs)

Release process: we deploy from main continuously. There are no release branches. Every merge to main triggers the CI/CD pipeline which deploys to staging automatically. Production deploys are triggered manually via ArgoCD approval.

Hotfix process: for urgent production fixes, branch from the latest deploy tag, fix, test, and merge directly to main. The fix deploys through the normal pipeline.

Protected branch rules on main:
- Require PR review (minimum 1 approval)
- Require status checks to pass (lint, test, build)
- No force pushes
- No deletion
- Linear history enforced (no merge commits)

Tag conventions: releases are tagged with semver (v1.2.3). Tags trigger the Docker image build and push to the registry. Changelog is auto-generated from conventional commit messages using release-please GitHub Action.

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
Frontend Coding Standards

These standards apply to all React/TypeScript code in the web and mobile applications.

TypeScript: strict mode is enabled. No any types except in test files. Use interface for object shapes and type for unions and intersections. Prefer discriminated unions over optional properties for state machines.

Component patterns:
- Functional components only (no class components)
- Props interface named ComponentNameProps, exported alongside the component
- Use React.forwardRef for components that wrap native elements
- Memoize with React.memo only when profiling shows a re-render problem, not preemptively
- Custom hooks prefixed with use- and placed in a hooks/ directory

State management:
- Server state: TanStack Query exclusively. No storing API responses in Zustand.
- Client state: Zustand with slices pattern. Each feature gets its own slice.
- Form state: React Hook Form with Zod schemas for validation
- URL state: use React Router's searchParams for filters and pagination

Styling: Tailwind CSS only. No inline styles, no CSS modules, no styled-components. Use the cn() utility (based on clsx + tailwind-merge) for conditional classes. Keep className strings under 100 characters; extract to a constant or component variant if longer.

Testing: Vitest for unit tests, React Testing Library for component tests. Test behavior, not implementation. Avoid testing internal state or implementation details.

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Team On-Call Rotation

We run a weekly on-call rotation across the backend team. The on-call engineer is the first responder for production alerts and the point of contact for urgent issues.

Schedule: on-call rotates every Monday at 9:00 AM UTC. The schedule is managed in PagerDuty with automatic handoff notifications. Each engineer does on-call approximately once every 5 weeks.

Responsibilities during on-call:
- Respond to P1/P2 PagerDuty alerts within 5/30 minutes respectively
- Monitor the #alerts Slack channel during business hours
- Perform the daily production health check: review Grafana dashboards for error rates, latency, and resource utilization
- Run the weekly database backup verification (restore to test instance and validate)
- Triage incoming support tickets that are tagged as "engineering"

Compensation: on-call engineers get a day off the following week (take it any day). If paged outside business hours, additional comp time is given.

Escalation path:
- Level 1: On-call engineer (PagerDuty)
- Level 2: Backend team lead (paged after 15 minutes without acknowledgment)
- Level 3: Engineering manager (paged after 30 minutes without acknowledgment)

Handoff protocol: outgoing on-call writes a brief summary in #oncall-handoff Slack channel documenting any ongoing issues, recent deployments to watch, and pending action items.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"entity","name":"PagerDuty"},{"type":"entity","name":"Slack"},{"type":"entity","name":"Grafana"},{"type":"concept","name":"On-Call Rotation"},{"type":"concept","name":"Escalation Path"},{"type":"concept","name":"Incident Response"},{"type":"concept","name":"Production Health Check"}],"edges":[{"source":"On-Call Rotation","target":"PagerDuty","relation":"uses"},{"source":"On-Call Rotation","target":"Slack","relation":"uses"},{"source":"On-Call Rotation","target":"Incident Response","relation":"related_to"},{"source":"Production Health Check","target":"Grafana","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Escalation Path","target":"PagerDuty","relation":"uses"},{"source":"On-Call Rotation","target":"Escalation Path","relation":"related_to"}]}

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Design System: Dark Mode Implementation

Our dark mode uses CSS custom properties with a class-based toggle, not prefers-color-scheme media query. This gives users explicit control independent of their OS setting.

Implementation: the html element gets a .dark class toggled by the ThemeProvider React component. The user's preference is persisted in localStorage under the key "theme" with values "light", "dark", or "system" (which then checks prefers-color-scheme at runtime).

Tailwind configuration: we use the class strategy (darkMode: 'class' in tailwind.config.ts). Dark mode colors are defined as CSS custom properties in globals.css:

:root { --background: 0 0% 100%; --foreground: 222 47% 11%; ... }
.dark { --background: 222 47% 11%; --foreground: 210 40% 98%; ... }

Tailwind maps these to color utilities: bg-background, text-foreground, etc. This avoids the dark: prefix explosion where every color class needs a dark: variant.

Component considerations: images need separate light and dark variants or an opacity overlay. Charts use a color palette that is accessible in both modes (verified with color contrast checkers). Code syntax highlighting themes switch between One Light and One Dark.

SSR handling: Next.js server-renders with no theme class, then the client-side ThemeProvider adds the class on hydration. A small inline script in the head prevents the flash of wrong theme by reading localStorage before React hydrates.

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Architecture Decision Record: ADR Template

All significant architectural decisions are documented as ADRs in the docs/adr/ directory. Every ADR follows this template:

Header: sequential number, title, date, status (proposed, accepted, deprecated, superseded)

Context: what is the technical problem or opportunity? Include constraints, requirements, and any deadlines.

Decision: what did we decide and why? Include alternatives that were considered and why they were rejected. Be specific about tools, libraries, patterns.

Consequences: what are the positive and negative outcomes? Include operational impact, migration effort, and ongoing maintenance cost.

Review process: ADRs are proposed as PRs to the docs/adr/ folder. The team discusses in the PR comments. Acceptance requires 2 approvals from senior engineers. Once merged, the decision is binding until a new ADR supersedes it.

Current ADRs cover: database choice (SQLite + sqlite-vec), embedding model selection (Ollama + nomic-embed-text), authentication mechanism (JWT with dual tokens), error handling convention (structured JSON errors), testing strategy (test pyramid with testcontainers), and logging standard (slog with JSON output).

We reference ADRs in code comments when a design choice might seem unusual. Format: // See ADR-007 for why we use SQLite instead of PostgreSQL.

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Security Coding Practices

Mandatory security practices for all developers. These are enforced via code review and automated scanning.

Input Validation:
- All API inputs are validated using go-playground/validator struct tags
- HTML content is sanitized using bluemonday before storage
- SQL queries use parameterized queries only — no string concatenation. sqlc and pgx enforce this
- File paths are validated against path traversal: reject any input containing .. or starting with /

Authentication & Authorization:
- JWT tokens are validated on every request by middleware (never skip for convenience)
- Use the PermissionChecker interface, never check roles directly
- API keys are hashed with bcrypt before storage, compared with constant-time functions
- Session tokens use crypto/rand for generation, never math/rand

Secrets:
- Never log secrets, tokens, passwords, or API keys
- Use the redact middleware to scrub sensitive fields from error responses
- Environment variables containing secrets must be prefixed with SECRET_ to trigger automatic redaction in logs
- All secrets in Kubernetes use sealed-secrets encrypted at rest

Dependencies:
- Snyk scans run on every PR to detect known vulnerabilities
- Go modules with critical CVEs must be updated within 48 hours
- No dependencies with AGPL or similar restrictive licenses (checked by license-checker in CI)

Audit: all authentication events (login, logout, token refresh, failed attempts) are logged to the immutable audit_log table.

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# New Engineer Onboarding: Frontend

Welcome to the frontend team. Here is how to get started with our web dashboard.

Prerequisites: Node.js 20 LTS installed via nvm, pnpm as the package manager (not npm or yarn). We use VS Code with the Tailwind CSS IntelliSense, ESLint, and Prettier extensions.

Setup:
1. Clone the repo: git clone git@github.com:example/web-dashboard.git
2. Install dependencies: pnpm install
3. Copy .env.local.example to .env.local
4. Start the dev server: pnpm dev (runs Next.js on port 3000)
5. Start Storybook: pnpm storybook (runs on port 6006)

Key directories:
- app/: Next.js App Router pages and layouts
- components/ui/: design system components (Button, Input, Dialog etc.)
- components/features/: feature-specific components (WorkspaceList, DocumentEditor)
- hooks/: custom React hooks
- lib/: utility functions and API client
- stores/: Zustand state slices

Development workflow:
- Pick a ticket from the "Ready for Dev" column in Linear
- Create a feature branch: git checkout -b feature/LIN-123-description
- Implement the feature with Storybook stories for new components
- Write tests with Vitest and React Testing Library
- Open a PR with screenshots or a Loom video showing the feature

Design files are in Figma. Each component in the design system has a Figma component with documented props and variants. When implementing a new design, compare your Storybook component with the Figma design pixel-by-pixel.

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Database Conventions and Guidelines

These conventions apply to all database schema design and query patterns.

Table naming: plural snake_case (users, workspace_members, api_keys). Junction tables: alpha-sorted combination (permission_roles, not role_permissions).

Column naming: snake_case. Primary keys: id (UUID v4, generated by the application). Timestamps: created_at, updated_at (TIMESTAMPTZ, set automatically by trigger), deleted_at (nullable, for soft deletes). Foreign keys: {referenced_table_singular}_id (e.g., user_id, workspace_id).

Indexes: every foreign key column gets an index. Composite indexes go in the order they are queried (most selective first). Partial indexes for soft-deleted records: WHERE deleted_at IS NULL. All indexes are created with CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY in migrations.

Migrations: managed by golang-migrate. Every change is a numbered pair (up/down). Never modify an applied migration. New columns start nullable, get backfilled, then add NOT NULL constraint in a separate migration.

Query patterns: use sqlc for all queries (generates type-safe Go code from SQL). Complex queries get a dedicated .sql file. Simple CRUD can use inline SQL in the sqlc annotations. Always include workspace_id in WHERE clauses for tenant isolation.

Performance: monitor slow queries via pg_stat_statements. Any query over 100ms gets investigated. Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE for optimization. Bulk operations use COPY instead of INSERT loops. Transactions should be as short as possible to minimize lock contention.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"decision","name":"Use sqlc for All Queries"},{"type":"decision","name":"UUID v4 Primary Keys"},{"type":"decision","name":"Concurrent Index Creation"},{"type":"entity","name":"sqlc"},{"type":"entity","name":"golang-migrate"},{"type":"entity","name":"pg_stat_statements"},{"type":"entity","name":"PostgreSQL"},{"type":"concept","name":"Database Conventions"},{"type":"concept","name":"Schema Migration"},{"type":"concept","name":"Multi-Tenant Isolation"},{"type":"concept","name":"Soft Delete"}],"edges":[{"source":"Use sqlc for All Queries","target":"sqlc","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use sqlc for All Queries","target":"Database Conventions","relation":"affects"},{"source":"UUID v4 Primary Keys","target":"PostgreSQL","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Concurrent Index Creation","target":"PostgreSQL","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Schema Migration","target":"golang-migrate","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Database Conventions","target":"Multi-Tenant Isolation","relation":"related_to"},{"source":"Database Conventions","target":"Soft Delete","relation":"related_to"},{"source":"Database Conventions","target":"pg_stat_statements","relation":"uses"}]}

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Accessibility Standards

Accessibility is not optional. Our target is WCAG 2.1 AA compliance across the entire application. This matters for enterprise customers with accessibility requirements in their procurement processes.

Keyboard navigation:
- All interactive elements must be reachable via Tab key
- Focus order follows visual order (no tabindex > 0)
- Custom components implement ARIA keyboard patterns (e.g., arrow keys in menus, Escape to close dialogs)
- Focus trap in modals and dialogs (handled by Radix UI's Dialog component)
- Skip-to-content link on every page

Screen readers:
- All images have alt text (decorative images use alt="")
- ARIA labels on icon-only buttons
- Live regions (aria-live) for dynamic content updates (toast notifications, search results)
- Semantic HTML: headings in order (h1 -> h2 -> h3), nav for navigation, main for content, aside for sidebars

Color and contrast:
- Text meets 4.5:1 contrast ratio (checked with axe-core and Lighthouse)
- Color is never the sole indicator of meaning (e.g., error states use both red color AND an icon/text)
- Focus rings are visible in both light and dark mode (2px solid with offset)

Testing:
- axe-core integration tests run on every Storybook story
- Manual screen reader testing with VoiceOver (macOS) and NVDA (Windows) before major releases
- Lighthouse accessibility score must be >= 95 in CI
- Every PR that touches UI components must include an accessibility impact note

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# API Style Guide

Our REST API follows consistent patterns for predictable client integration.

URL structure: resources are plural nouns (/v2/workspaces, /v2/documents). Sub-resources use nesting: /v2/workspaces/:id/members. Actions that do not map to CRUD use POST with a verb: POST /v2/documents/:id/archive.

HTTP methods: GET for reads (idempotent), POST for creates, PATCH for partial updates (JSON Merge Patch), PUT for full replacement (rarely used), DELETE for removal.

Response format: all responses return JSON. Success responses include a data field with the resource or collection. Error responses include an error object with code, message, and request_id. Collections include pagination metadata: total_count, has_more, next_cursor.

Pagination: cursor-based for consistency and performance. Clients pass ?cursor=xxx&limit=25. The response includes next_cursor if there are more results. We avoid offset-based pagination because it breaks when items are inserted or deleted between pages.

Filtering: query parameters use the pattern field=value for equality, field_gte=value and field_lte=value for ranges, and field_in=val1,val2 for multiple values. Complex filters use a JSON-encoded ?filter= parameter.

Versioning: URL-based with /v1/, /v2/ prefixes. Deprecated endpoints return Sunset and Deprecation headers. New versions are introduced for breaking changes only; additive changes are made in-place.

Idempotency: POST and PATCH requests support an Idempotency-Key header. Keys are stored in Redis with a 24-hour TTL. Repeated requests with the same key return the cached response.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"decision","name":"Use Cursor-Based Pagination"},{"type":"decision","name":"Support Idempotency Keys"},{"type":"decision","name":"Use JSON Merge Patch"},{"type":"entity","name":"Redis"},{"type":"entity","name":"REST API"},{"type":"concept","name":"API Style Guide"},{"type":"concept","name":"URL-Based Versioning"},{"type":"concept","name":"Idempotency"},{"type":"concept","name":"Cursor-Based Pagination"}],"edges":[{"source":"Use Cursor-Based Pagination","target":"Cursor-Based Pagination","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Use Cursor-Based Pagination","target":"REST API","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Support Idempotency Keys","target":"Redis","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Support Idempotency Keys","target":"Idempotency","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Use JSON Merge Patch","target":"REST API","relation":"affects"},{"source":"API Style Guide","target":"URL-Based Versioning","relation":"related_to"},{"source":"API Style Guide","target":"REST API","relation":"affects"}]}

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Infrastructure as Code Standards

All infrastructure is defined in code and version-controlled. No manual changes to production infrastructure. This is non-negotiable.

Terraform:
- All cloud resources (AWS, Fly.io) are managed by Terraform modules in the infra/ repository
- Modules follow the standard structure: variables.tf, main.tf, outputs.tf
- State is stored in an S3 bucket with DynamoDB locking for team workflows
- terraform plan runs on every PR via GitHub Actions; terraform apply runs on merge to main with manual approval
- Sensitive values are passed via Terraform Cloud variables, never in .tf files

Kubernetes:
- Manifests are templated with Helm charts stored in the deploy/ directory of each service repo
- Environment-specific overrides use Kustomize overlays (dev, staging, prod)
- ArgoCD watches the GitOps repo for changes and syncs automatically

Drift detection:
- Terraform Cloud runs a nightly plan-only job to detect drift between state and actual infrastructure
- Drift alerts go to the #infra Slack channel
- Any detected drift must be resolved by importing the change into Terraform, never by reverting the infrastructure manually

Secret management: Terraform provisions the Vault paths and policies. Application secrets are managed in Vault and injected at runtime, never stored in Terraform state.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"decision","name":"All Infrastructure Defined in Code"},{"type":"decision","name":"Nightly Drift Detection"},{"type":"entity","name":"Terraform"},{"type":"entity","name":"Terraform Cloud"},{"type":"entity","name":"Amazon S3"},{"type":"entity","name":"DynamoDB"},{"type":"entity","name":"Helm"},{"type":"entity","name":"Kustomize"},{"type":"entity","name":"ArgoCD"},{"type":"entity","name":"HashiCorp Vault"},{"type":"entity","name":"GitHub Actions"},{"type":"concept","name":"Infrastructure as Code"},{"type":"concept","name":"Drift Detection"},{"type":"concept","name":"GitOps"}],"edges":[{"source":"All Infrastructure Defined in Code","target":"Infrastructure as Code","relation":"affects"},{"source":"All Infrastructure Defined in Code","target":"Terraform","relation":"uses"},{"source":"All Infrastructure Defined in Code","target":"Helm","relation":"uses"},{"source":"All Infrastructure Defined in Code","target":"Kustomize","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Nightly Drift Detection","target":"Drift Detection","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Nightly Drift Detection","target":"Terraform Cloud","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Infrastructure as Code","target":"GitOps","relation":"related_to"},{"source":"GitOps","target":"ArgoCD","relation":"uses"},{"source":"All Infrastructure Defined in Code","target":"GitHub Actions","relation":"uses"},{"source":"All Infrastructure Defined in Code","target":"HashiCorp Vault","relation":"related_to"}]}

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Error Reporting and Triage Process

We use Sentry for application error tracking across all services. Every unhandled exception and every error logged at ERROR level is automatically reported to Sentry with full context.

Sentry configuration:
- Each service has its own Sentry project for clear ownership
- The Go SDK captures panics, HTTP handler errors, and explicitly reported errors
- Every event includes: user_id, workspace_id, request_id, service version, and Go runtime info
- Source maps are uploaded for the frontend during the build step
- PII scrubbing is enabled: email addresses, IP addresses, and auth tokens are redacted before sending

Triage process:
- New errors auto-create issues in Sentry and post to the #errors Slack channel
- The on-call engineer reviews new errors daily and assigns ownership
- Issues are linked to GitHub issues for tracking via Sentry's GitHub integration
- Resolved issues auto-reopen if the error recurs in a new release

Grouping: Sentry groups errors by stack trace fingerprint. We use custom fingerprinting for errors that share a stack trace but differ by input (e.g., validation errors grouped by field name, not by the specific invalid value).

Performance: Sentry traces are sampled at 10% in production, 100% in staging. Critical transactions (auth, payment) are always traced regardless of sample rate using custom sampling rules.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"decision","name":"Use Sentry for Error Tracking"},{"type":"decision","name":"Custom Error Fingerprinting"},{"type":"entity","name":"Sentry"},{"type":"entity","name":"Slack"},{"type":"entity","name":"GitHub"},{"type":"entity","name":"Go"},{"type":"concept","name":"Error Tracking"},{"type":"concept","name":"PII Scrubbing"},{"type":"concept","name":"Trace Sampling"}],"edges":[{"source":"Use Sentry for Error Tracking","target":"Sentry","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use Sentry for Error Tracking","target":"Error Tracking","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Custom Error Fingerprinting","target":"Sentry","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Error Tracking","target":"Slack","relation":"related_to"},{"source":"Error Tracking","target":"GitHub","relation":"related_to"},{"source":"Use Sentry for Error Tracking","target":"PII Scrubbing","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Trace Sampling","target":"Sentry","relation":"related_to"},{"source":"Use Sentry for Error Tracking","target":"Go","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Error Tracking","target":"Trace Sampling","relation":"related_to"}]}

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Pair Programming and Mob Programming Guidelines

We use pair and mob programming for complex or high-risk tasks. Not mandatory for all work, but encouraged when:
- Working on a new feature with unfamiliar domain logic
- Debugging a production incident (mob programming for faster resolution)
- Onboarding new team members (pair with an experienced engineer)
- Tackling tech debt that spans multiple services

Pair programming setup: both engineers use VS Code Live Share for real-time collaborative editing. The driver writes code while the navigator reviews and thinks ahead. Switch roles every 25 minutes (Pomodoro style). Both engineers commit as co-authors using Git's Co-authored-by trailer.

Mob programming: the entire backend team (3-5 people) works on one screen for 2-hour sessions. One person drives (10-minute rotations), others navigate. We use this for architecture decisions that affect everyone and for post-incident fixes where we want shared understanding.

Remote tooling:
- VS Code Live Share for code collaboration
- Tuple or Slack Huddle for screen sharing and voice
- Miro for whiteboarding architecture diagrams during mob sessions

Productivity tip: keep sessions under 2 hours. After that, diminishing returns set in. Schedule a 15-minute break before continuing. For pair programming, alternate between pairing and solo work throughout the day.

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Design System: Form Patterns

Forms are one of the most complex UI patterns in our application. Here is how we standardize them.

Form state management: all forms use React Hook Form for state, validation, and submission handling. Schema validation uses Zod with automatic TypeScript type inference. The schema is defined once and used for both client-side validation and API request typing.

Example pattern:
const schema = z.object({ name: z.string().min(1), email: z.string().email() })
type FormData = z.infer<typeof schema>
const form = useForm<FormData>({ resolver: zodResolver(schema) })

Form components:
- FormField: wrapper that connects React Hook Form to our Input/Select/Checkbox components via the Controller API
- FormMessage: displays validation errors below the field with aria-describedby for accessibility
- FormLabel: associates labels with inputs and shows required indicators

Layout patterns:
- Single column for simple forms (login, settings)
- Two column for data-heavy forms (workspace creation, user profile)
- Multi-step wizard using a Stepper component with form state persisted across steps via Zustand

Submission handling: the form's onSubmit calls a TanStack Query mutation. Loading state shows a spinner in the submit button (disabled to prevent double-submit). Success shows a toast notification via Sonner. Errors from the API are mapped back to individual fields using setError if the API returns field-level errors.

Optimistic updates: for inline edits (like renaming a document), we use TanStack Query's optimistic update pattern to show the change immediately and revert on error.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"decision","name":"Standardize Forms with React Hook Form"},{"type":"decision","name":"Use Zod for Schema Validation"},{"type":"entity","name":"React Hook Form"},{"type":"entity","name":"Zod"},{"type":"entity","name":"TanStack Query"},{"type":"entity","name":"Zustand"},{"type":"entity","name":"Sonner"},{"type":"concept","name":"Form Patterns"},{"type":"concept","name":"Schema Validation"},{"type":"concept","name":"Optimistic Updates"},{"type":"concept","name":"Accessibility"}],"edges":[{"source":"Standardize Forms with React Hook Form","target":"React Hook Form","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Standardize Forms with React Hook Form","target":"Form Patterns","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Use Zod for Schema Validation","target":"Zod","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use Zod for Schema Validation","target":"Schema Validation","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Form Patterns","target":"TanStack Query","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Form Patterns","target":"Zustand","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Optimistic Updates","target":"TanStack Query","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Form Patterns","target":"Sonner","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Form Patterns","target":"Accessibility","relation":"related_to"}]}

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Release Process Documentation

We follow a continuous delivery model where every merge to main is potentially deployable. Here is the full release lifecycle.

Versioning: we use semantic versioning (major.minor.patch). The version is managed by release-please, a GitHub Action that auto-generates changelogs and bumps versions based on conventional commit messages. feat: commits bump the minor version, fix: commits bump the patch version, and BREAKING CHANGE in the commit body bumps the major version.

Release flow:
1. Developers merge PRs to main following the PR process
2. release-please creates a Release PR that accumulates changes since the last release
3. The Release PR auto-updates with each merge, showing the changelog diff
4. When ready to release, a maintainer merges the Release PR
5. This triggers GitHub Actions to: create a Git tag, create a GitHub Release with changelog, build the Docker image tagged with the version, push to Amazon ECR, and update the Helm chart version in the GitOps repo
6. ArgoCD detects the Helm chart change and deploys to staging automatically
7. After staging validation, a maintainer approves the production sync in ArgoCD

Rollback: if the release causes issues, revert to the previous version by clicking "Sync to Previous" in ArgoCD. This is instant. For a code-level revert, create a revert commit and go through the normal release process.

npm distribution: the CLI binary is also published to npm as the same package. The npm publish step runs after the Docker build in the same GitHub Actions workflow. The npm package is a thin wrapper that downloads the correct platform binary.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"decision","name":"Use release-please for Versioning"},{"type":"decision","name":"Continuous Delivery Model"},{"type":"entity","name":"release-please"},{"type":"entity","name":"GitHub Actions"},{"type":"entity","name":"Amazon ECR"},{"type":"entity","name":"ArgoCD"},{"type":"entity","name":"Helm"},{"type":"entity","name":"Docker"},{"type":"entity","name":"npm"},{"type":"concept","name":"Semantic Versioning"},{"type":"concept","name":"Continuous Delivery"},{"type":"concept","name":"Conventional Commits"},{"type":"concept","name":"GitOps"}],"edges":[{"source":"Use release-please for Versioning","target":"release-please","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use release-please for Versioning","target":"Semantic Versioning","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Use release-please for Versioning","target":"Conventional Commits","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Continuous Delivery Model","target":"Continuous Delivery","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Continuous Delivery Model","target":"GitHub Actions","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Continuous Delivery Model","target":"ArgoCD","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Continuous Delivery","target":"GitOps","relation":"related_to"},{"source":"GitHub Actions","target":"Amazon ECR","relation":"uses"},{"source":"GitHub Actions","target":"Docker","relation":"uses"},{"source":"GitHub Actions","target":"npm","relation":"uses"},{"source":"GitOps","target":"Helm","relation":"uses"}]}
